text.title: Three Mom Test rules, good vs bad questions, and commitment signals
text.summary: Three rules eliminate the politeness bias in customer interviews: past behavior over hypotheticals, their life over your idea, listening over pitching.

rule.statement: Rule 1: Ask about their life and problems, not about your idea or solution. Never mention your idea until you have established whether the problem is real.
rule.rationale: When you mention your idea, the interview becomes an evaluation of you, not a discovery of their needs. Polite respondents optimize for not hurting your feelings.

rule.statement: Rule 2: Ask about past specific behavior, not future hypotheticals. "Would you..." and "Do you think..." produce answers based on what people wish they did, not what they actually do.
rule.rationale: Past behavior is the best predictor of future behavior. Hypothetical behavior reflects self-concept, which routinely diverges from actual behavior.

rule.statement: Rule 3: Listen more than 80% of the time. If you are talking more than 20% of the time, you are pitching, not learning.
rule.rationale: Pitching during discovery contaminates the data — the interviewee starts responding to your framing instead of describing their actual experience.

list.item: Bad: "Do you think this is a good idea?" → Good: "How do you currently solve this?"
Bad: "Would you buy this?" → Good: "When did you last pay for a solution to this problem?"
Bad: "Would you use this feature?" → Good: "Tell me about the last time you had this problem"
Bad: "How much would you pay?" → Good: "What do you currently spend on this?"
Bad: "Do you have this problem?" (leading) → Good: "Walk me through your workflow"

rule.statement: Real validation requires escalating commitment: Time commitment (beta test agreement) is the minimum bar; Reputation commitment (introduction to a colleague or CTO) is stronger; Money commitment (deposit, prepayment, pilot contract) is the strongest signal.
rule.rationale: Verbal enthusiasm is costless and therefore uninformative. Commitment costs the interviewee something real, so it reflects genuine belief in the value.

list.item: Compliment (worthless): "I would definitely use this", "Great idea!", "Interesting concept"
Time (minimum): "Can I be a beta tester?", "Schedule a pilot next month"
Reputation: "I'll introduce you to our CTO", "I'll send you our requirements doc"
Money (strongest): "$50 deposit", "We'll sign an LOI", "I'll prepay for the first year"
"I would..." — hypothetical, not real; probe for a specific past instance
"I usually..." — generic pattern; probe for the last specific time
"I think..." — opinion, not behavior; ask for a concrete example
Compliments without follow-up questions from them — signals low interest
Vague answers — signals they have not actually experienced the problem
